# Motivation {#sec-motivation .unnumbered}

> **§1.** *Conceptual; no software. Motivates the measurement problem, fixes
> subjective expected utility (SEU) maximization as the stated standard, and
> states the contribution and roadmap. The formal development begins in
> §[2](#sec-abstract-model).*

## 1.1 Two questions about a decision {#sec-two-questions .unnumbered}

There are two distinct questions one can ask about a decision. The first is
*external*: did the choice match the outcome we cared about --- did the claim
that was forwarded turn out to be fraudulent, did the gamble that was taken pay
off? The second is *procedural*: did the choice have the right *internal
structure* relative to a stated standard --- was it responsive to the
probabilities, the stakes, and the tradeoffs in the way the standard
prescribes? The two questions can come apart. A decision can be procedurally
impeccable and still turn out badly, because the world is uncertain; and a
decision can be procedurally incoherent and still turn out well, because luck
is not selective. When we evaluate a decision maker --- a human analyst, an
institution, or, increasingly, a large language model deployed to make or
recommend choices --- we usually care about the second question, even when only
the first is easy to score.

## 1.2 Why labeled accuracy under-determines decision quality {#sec-why-labels-fail .unnumbered}

The natural way to evaluate choices is to score them against labels: known
correct answers, realized outcomes, or expert adjudications. For procedural
evaluation this is often unavailable or misleading. Labels may be simply
*absent* --- many consequential choices are made in domains where no
ground-truth key exists. They may be *costly* --- obtaining an outcome can
require running an expensive experiment or waiting out a long horizon. They may
be *judgment-dependent* --- the "correct" label is itself a contested expert
opinion rather than a fact. And, most fundamentally, they may be *confounded
with luck*: a decision under uncertainty is made *before* the uncertainty
resolves, while the outcome is observed *after*, so any outcome-based score
conflates the quality of the decision with the realization of chance. An agent
that reliably makes well-structured choices will still accumulate bad outcomes
at a rate set by the irreducible noise of its environment. Scoring against
realized outcomes therefore measures a mixture of competence and fortune, and
the mixing weights are exactly the thing we cannot observe.

## 1.3 The need for procedural evaluation {#sec-procedural .unnumbered}

What survives the absence of clean labels is the *structure* of the choices
themselves. If we are willing to state a standard of good decision making
explicitly, we can ask whether an agent's choices are responsive to
probabilities, utilities, and tradeoffs in the way that standard prescribes ---
without ever needing to know which choice was, in hindsight, correct.
Procedural evaluation trades a claim about outcomes for a claim about
conformity to a stated norm. The price of admission is that one must name the
norm; the payoff is an evaluation that is well defined even where no outcome key
exists, and that is not contaminated by the luck of the draw.

## 1.4 SEU as the stated standard {#sec-seu-standard .unnumbered}

We take *subjective expected utility (SEU) maximization* as the reference
standard. An SEU-committed agent holds a subjective probability over the states
of the world and a utility over consequences, and prefers the alternative whose
expected utility is greatest [@savage1954; @anscombe1963definition;
@vonneumann1947]. We do *not* argue that SEU is the uniquely correct theory of
rational choice, and nothing below depends on that stronger claim. The argument
is conditional: *if* SEU is the reference standard, then a graded, statistically
rigorous measure of conformity to it is methodologically useful. SEU is the
natural first case for such an instrument for two reasons. It is the most widely
shared normative benchmark in decision theory, so a conformity measure built on
it speaks to the largest audience; and its likelihood structure is the
best understood, which makes it the cleanest setting in which to ask the
identifiability questions that occupy §§[3](#sec-m0-identifiability) and
[5](#sec-m1-identifiability). Parallel instruments for non-SEU standards ---
rank-dependent utility [@tversky1992], cumulative prospect theory
[@kahneman1979], or maxmin expected utility [@gilboa1989] --- are possible by
the same template and are out of scope here.

## 1.5 Why a graded measure rather than a binary verdict {#sec-graded .unnumbered}

Real decision makers, human or machine, only ever *approximately* satisfy any
rationality axiom [@simon1955; @lieder2020]. A binary verdict --- "rational" or
"not" --- discards exactly the information that an evaluation should preserve,
namely *how closely* and *how reliably* an agent's choices track the standard. We
therefore measure conformity on a continuum. The formal vehicle, developed in
§[2](#sec-abstract-model), is a softmax (Luce--McFadden) choice model
[@luce1959; @mcfadden1974; @train2009] carrying a single scalar *sensitivity*
parameter $\alpha \geq 0$ applied to SEU-valued alternatives. As $\alpha$ ranges
from $0$ to $\infty$, the implied behavior runs from uniform-random choice
(insensitive to expected utility) to deterministic SEU maximization (perfectly
sensitive to it). The empirically interesting agents live strictly between these
poles, and $\alpha$ is the coordinate that locates them there. We call $\alpha$
the agent's **SEU sensitivity**: the disposition of an agent committed to SEU
maximization to act in accordance with that commitment, held separate from both
the *content* of the commitment (the agent's beliefs and utilities) and the
*outcomes* it happens to realize.

## 1.6 Scope {#sec-scope .unnumbered}

This paper is methodological. Its center of gravity is a calibrated measurement
instrument and the identifiability analysis that licenses it; the
decision-theoretic and philosophical framing is a register, not the disciplinary
home. §§[2](#sec-abstract-model)--[6](#sec-m1-implementation) specify, motivate,
and validate the instrument: the abstract choice model and its three
characterizing properties (§[2](#sec-abstract-model)), the identifiability of
$\alpha$ together with the *weak* identifiability of the belief and utility
parameters $(\beta, \delta)$ in an uncertain-choice-only model
(§[3](#sec-m0-identifiability)), a Stan implementation validated by prior
predictive checks, parameter recovery, and simulation-based calibration (SBC)
(§[4](#sec-m0-implementation)), and the same arc for an extended model that adds
risky choices (§§[5](#sec-m1-identifiability)--[6](#sec-m1-implementation)).
§[7](#sec-application) then runs the full workflow end-to-end on real LLM choice
data: a $2\times2$ illustrative application crossing GPT-4o and Claude 3.5 Sonnet
with insurance-claims triage and Ellsberg-style urn gambles
[@ellsberg1961], using sampling temperature as the experimental lever. The
application is included to show that the methodology does real evaluative work on
real choice data [@binz2023; @hagendorff2023]; it is *not* a substantive
contribution to LLM behavioral science, and §[7.6](#sec-app-limits) draws that
line explicitly.

Several things are deliberately out of scope, and are flagged once at their
natural locations rather than revisited: an information-optimal lottery design
for sharpening the utility parameter (future work, §[8.5](#sec-disc-limitations)); the
hierarchical extension `h_m01` and the multi-LLM alignment study it was built for
(a planned companion paper, §[7.7](#sec-app-followup)); generalized-sensitivity
models with block-specific or context-specific $\alpha$; a test for ambiguity
aversion in the Ellsberg setting (the SEU-plus-softmax model is, by assumption,
of the wrong form for that, §[7.6](#sec-app-limits)); and any absolute-rationality
ranking *between* LLMs (only within-design comparative claims about $\alpha$ are
licensed). The target length is roughly 35--45 pages (about 25 of body plus
appendices), a budget that disciplines the application's expansiveness in
particular.

## 1.7 What is new {#sec-contribution .unnumbered}

Multinomial logit is McFadden's [@mcfadden1974]; Stan, SBC, and the
prior-predictive/recovery/SBC workflow have been standard practice for a decade
[@carpenter2017; @talts2018; @gelman2020]. The contribution here is not any one
of these tools but the following four-part package.

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
**(a) A clean identifiability decomposition.** We separate the identifiability
of $\alpha$ *from the expected-utility vector* $\eta$ --- stated as a proposition
under an explicit genericity condition (§[3.3](#sec-alpha-from-eta), Appendix
B.1) --- from the *ridge* identifiability of $(\beta, \delta)$, stated as a
**numerically-supported proposition**: a Fisher-information / near-kernel claim
verified by direct computation (§[3.4](#sec-bd-weak), Appendix B.2), *not* a
strict invariance theorem. The two facets are one structural picture
(§[3.5](#sec-m0-link)): $\alpha$ is read off the curvature of choice
probabilities in $\eta$, while beliefs and utilities enter only *through* $\eta$
and trade off along a low-curvature ridge that uncertain-choice data barely
resolves.

**(b) A worked demonstration that in-principle identifiability does not predict
finite-sample estimability.** The `m_0`/`m_1` contrast makes the gap concrete in
two distinct ways. For the utility parameter $\delta$, adding the risky block
makes it identifiable in principle (§[5.5](#sec-delta-id)) yet buys almost no
precision at the design sample size --- a matched-count credible-interval-width
reduction under 1% (§[6.4](#sec-m1-recovery)). For the sensitivity parameter
$\alpha$, a matched recovery study shows that finite-sample precision is governed
by data *quantity*, not by the *type* of choice that an in-principle argument
might privilege. A first-pass recovery study at $n = 30$ reported a tidy
$\approx 15\%$ $\alpha$ gain attributable to choice type; a pre-registered re-run
at $n = 100$ reversed it. We report that reversal as a cautionary instance of the
very discipline the paper advocates.

**(c) The marginal-SBC demarcation.** Marginal rank uniformity is *necessary but
not sufficient* for joint posterior calibration: a ridge in the joint
$(\beta, \delta)$ posterior is invisible to per-parameter SBC, which passes for
both models even where the joint belief--utility posterior is only weakly
identified (§§[4.4](#sec-m0-sbc), [6.5](#sec-m1-sbc), [8.3](#sec-discussion)).

**(d) An honestly reported full-pipeline application** of the instrument to LLM
choice data, in which every validation diagnostic comes in clean and the
framework detects a structured comparative effect in two of four LLM$\times$task
cells while declining to support one in the other two
(§[7](#sec-application)).
:::

## 1.8 Roadmap {#sec-roadmap .unnumbered}

The paper alternates between *abstract/identifiability* sections and their
*computational* realizations, then applies the result. §[2](#sec-abstract-model)
fixes the abstract model. §[3](#sec-m0-identifiability) establishes the
identifiability picture for the uncertain-choice-only model `m_0`, and
§[4](#sec-m0-implementation) realizes and validates it in Stan.
§[5](#sec-m1-identifiability) extends the model with risky choices and revisits
identifiability, and §[6](#sec-m1-implementation) realizes and validates the
extension --- inheriting both the design and the identifiability questions from
the earlier pair. §[7](#sec-application) runs the validated workflow on real LLM
data, and §[8](#sec-discussion-top) draws the methodological lessons together.
The formal backbone --- the proofs that close §§[3](#sec-m0-identifiability) and
[5](#sec-m1-identifiability) --- lives in Appendix B; the body states results and
points to it. Concretely, the dependency structure is

$$
\underbrace{\S2}_{\text{model}}
\;\longrightarrow\;
\underbrace{\S3 \to \S4}_{\texttt{m\_0}}
\;\longrightarrow\;
\underbrace{\S5 \to \S6}_{\texttt{m\_1}}
\;\longrightarrow\;
\underbrace{\S7}_{\text{application}}
\;\longrightarrow\;
\underbrace{\S8}_{\text{discussion}},
$$

with §[3](#sec-m0-identifiability) feeding *both* §[4](#sec-m0-implementation) and
§[5](#sec-m1-identifiability), the two implementation sections
(§§[4](#sec-m0-implementation), [6](#sec-m1-implementation)) converging on the
application, and Appendix B supplying the proofs that close the two
identifiability sections.
